articles.title: Astronauts Return Safely from a Trip Around the Moon
articles.summary: Four astronauts came back to Earth after a daring trip around the Moon. They flew in a spacecraft called Orion, which landed in the Pacific Ocean near San Diego. The astronauts—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen—were picked up by a Navy ship and are all healthy and happy. This mission, called Artemis II, was the first time people have gone to the Moon in over 50 years. They didn't land on the Moon but took amazing pictures and set a record for being the farthest humans have ever traveled from Earth. NASA plans to send more missions to land on the Moon soon.

articles.title: A Trip Around the Moon: What's Next?
articles.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission was a big success! Four astronauts flew around the Moon and came back safely in the Orion spacecraft. They took amazing pictures that made many kids excited about space. But going to the Moon is just the first step. The real challenge is to build a place where people can live and work on the Moon, like a Moon base. NASA plans to land astronauts on the Moon every year starting in 2028. However, building the special landers needed for this is taking longer than expected. Companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin are working on these landers, but they are behind schedule. So, while the mission was a triumph, there is still a lot of hard work ahead to make living on the Moon possible.

articles.title: How an Old Plate Powers Yellowstone's Volcanoes
articles.summary: Yellowstone is a special place in North America with big volcanoes that sometimes erupt and cover the land with ash. Scientists used to think a hot blob from deep inside Earth, called a mantle plume, made these volcanoes. But a new study says something else might be causing them. Long ago, a giant plate called the Farallon plate pushed under North America and helped build the West Coast. Even though most of this plate is gone now, it left behind stresses in the ground. These stresses open up paths for hot, melted rock to travel up from deep below and reach the surface at Yellowstone. This idea explains why Yellowstone has volcanoes even though it's not near the edge of a plate. The study uses a model to show how the rock moves through the crust, with two branches feeding different areas. This helps us understand Earth's history and how volcanoes work.

articles.title: A Fossil Mistake: Not an Octopus After All
articles.summary: Scientists found a fossil called Pohlsepia that was thought to be the oldest octopus, but it turned out to be something else. This fossil was discovered in Illinois and is about 311 to 306 million years old. For many years, people believed it was an octopus because it looked like one with a head, arms, and spots for eyes. But new tests using special machines showed it was actually a nautiloid, which is a different sea creature with a shell. The fossil was hard to see because it was just a flat stain on a rock, making it tricky to identify. This discovery helps us learn more about how sea animals evolved long ago.

articles.title: A New Way to Help People with Alzheimer's Disease
articles.summary: Alzheimer's disease is a sickness that makes it hard for people to remember things and think clearly, especially as they get older. Scientists have been trying to find medicines to help, but they haven't worked very well because Alzheimer's is caused by many different problems in the brain and body. A new review by Professor Yan-Jiang Wang and his team says we need to look at all these problems together. For example, there are sticky proteins called amyloid-beta and tau that build up in the brain, and genes can make some people more likely to get the disease. Also, getting older and having other health issues like diabetes can make Alzheimer's worse. The scientists think that future treatments should target multiple things at once, like using gene therapy or medicines for diabetes, to slow down the disease better. They also want to use better tests to find Alzheimer's early so doctors can help sooner.

articles.title: Finding Space Ripples in Tiny Atoms
articles.summary: Scientists have a new idea to find gravitational waves, which are tiny ripples in space made by big events like black holes crashing together. Usually, they use huge machines that are kilometers long to detect these waves. But now, researchers think we can look at how atoms, the tiny building blocks of everything, give off light. When atoms release light, gravitational waves might change the color of that light depending on which way it goes. This could help us learn about the waves without needing giant tools. The idea is still just a theory and hasn't been tested yet, but it might lead to smaller detectors in the future, making it easier to study space.
